event: Austronesian-speaking Lapita peoples voyaging from the Bismarck Archipelago reach the Fijian islands, marking the first human settlement of the archipelago.
Lapita communities established distinctive dentate-stamped pottery, horticulture, and fishing-based village societies across coastal Fiji.
Later Polynesian and Melanesian interactions and migrations blend cultures, and Fiji becomes a hub linking the settlement of Tonga and Samoa to the east.
Complex chiefly societies develop with fortified hill villages, large communal canoes, and extensive trade and warfare networks among islands.
Powerful confederations such as Bau, Rewa, Verata, and Cakaudrove emerge, dominating Fijian politics through alliances, tribute, and warfare.
Dutch navigator Abel Tasman becomes the first European to sight the Fijian islands, charting dangerous reefs in the northeast group.
British explorer Captain James Cook sights and visits the southern Lau islands during his Pacific voyages.
Captain William Bligh navigates through the Fiji islands in an open boat after the mutiny on the Bounty, charting waters later called the Bligh Water.
The wreck of the American ship Argo introduces European weapons and diseases, intensifying inter-tribal warfare and a devastating dysentery epidemic.
Sandalwood traders arrive in the Bua Bay region, beginning sustained European commercial contact that supplies muskets to rival chiefs.
Levuka on Ovalau island is established as the first European settlement and becomes Fiji's main trading port.
Beche-de-mer (sea cucumber) trading with China booms, drawing more Europeans and further arming the coastal chiefdoms.
Wesleyan Methodist missionaries from Tonga arrive at Lakeba, beginning the Christian conversion of the Fijian islands.
Ratu Seru Epenisa Cakobau, the paramount chief of Bau, converts to Christianity, accelerating the spread of Methodism across Fiji.
With Tongan military help under Enele Maafu, Cakobau defeats his rivals at the Battle of Kaba, consolidating his dominance.
Rival chiefs form a short-lived Confederacy of Independent Kingdoms of Fiji in an early attempt at unified governance.
Cakobau is proclaimed King of Fiji with a national government seated at Levuka, though it struggles with debt and instability.
Facing chaos and debt, Cakobau and other chiefs cede Fiji to Britain through the Deed of Cession, making it a British Crown Colony.
A measles epidemic, brought back by chiefs visiting Sydney, kills roughly a quarter to a third of the indigenous Fijian population.
The first indentured laborers from British India arrive aboard the Leonidas to work on sugar plantations under the girmit system.
The Colonial Sugar Refining Company begins operations, establishing sugar as the colony's dominant export industry.
The colonial capital is moved from Levuka to the better-harbored town of Suva.
A Legislative Council is reformed to include elected European members alongside nominated officials, while Fijians remain represented through chiefs.
The indentured labor (girmit) recruitment system is halted amid mounting criticism of its harsh conditions.
Indentured contracts are fully abolished, and many Indo-Fijians settle permanently as free farmers and merchants.
Fiji becomes a key Allied base and staging area in the Pacific theatre of World War II, with Fijian soldiers later serving with distinction in the Solomon Islands.
Corporal Sefanaia Sukanaivalu is posthumously awarded the Victoria Cross for valor during fighting on Bougainville.
Women and indigenous Fijians gain the right to vote, and the franchise is broadened in colonial elections.
A new constitution introduces a largely elected Legislative Council, advancing Fiji toward self-government.
Fiji gains full independence from Britain on 10 October, with Ratu Sir Kamisese Mara as its first Prime Minister.
Fiji is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state on 13 October.
Lieutenant Colonel Sitiveni Rabuka stages two military coups, overthrowing an Indo-Fijian-supported government and declaring Fiji a republic, leading to its expulsion from the Commonwealth.
A new constitution entrenches indigenous Fijian political dominance, prompting large-scale Indo-Fijian emigration.
A reformed multiracial constitution is adopted, and Fiji is readmitted to the Commonwealth of Nations.
Businessman George Speight leads an armed coup, taking Prime Minister Mahendra Chaudhry and others hostage in parliament.
Military commander Commodore Frank Bainimarama seizes power in a coup, ousting the elected government of Laisenia Qarase.
Fiji's 1997 constitution is abrogated, the judiciary is dismissed, and Fiji is suspended from the Commonwealth and the Pacific Islands Forum.
A new constitution is promulgated, establishing a single common electoral roll and equal citizenship for all Fijians.
Fiji holds its first democratic general election since the 2006 coup, with Bainimarama's FijiFirst party winning and the country rejoining the Commonwealth.
Severe Tropical Cyclone Winston, the strongest storm ever recorded in the Southern Hemisphere, devastates Fiji, killing dozens and causing massive damage.
Fiji becomes the first developing nation to preside over a UN climate change conference, presiding over COP23 in Bonn and championing low-lying island states.
General elections lead to a coalition government, and Sitiveni Rabuka returns as Prime Minister, ending Bainimarama's long tenure.
Fiji continues relocating vulnerable coastal villages inland as rising seas, erosion, and saltwater intrusion threaten low-lying communities.
